serving.natural_language_portion: One typical bowl of pork kidney meesua soup weighs approximately 400 g, comprising broth, wheat vermicelli noodles, sliced pork kidney, and green vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Kidney Meesua Soup — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Kidney Meesua Soup (cr_id F05060101). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — wheat vermicelli / thin wheat noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork kidney meesua soup as a composite dish. Meesua (thin wheat vermicelli) is the dominant carbohydrate source. GI of 53 is estimated from thin wheat vermicelli / wheat noodle entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which report GI values of 45–58 for thin wheat noodles. A midpoint of 53 is used, which falls into the 'low' GI category (55 or less). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 26.4) / 100 ≈ 14.0, based on 400 g serving with 6.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 203 mg per 100 g; typical 400 g bowl delivers approximately 812 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the 2000 mg daily limit, with additional sodium from soy sauce and broth.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit soup broth consumption, request reduced-sodium soy sauce, and keep total bowl intake to a smaller portion to stay within daily sodium targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Meesua is a refined wheat vermicelli with low dietary fibre (0.5 g per 100 g) and an estimated low GI of 53; a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 26 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~14.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion with more kidney and vegetables to reduce glycemic load, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the refined-starch base and low fibre content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork kidney is an organ meat high in cholesterol and arachidonic acid; combined with moderate sodium (203 mg per 100 g) and a typical large serving size, cumulative sodium and dietary cholesterol intake may be clinically significant.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of organ-meat dishes, avoid consuming the full broth, and choose smaller portions to manage sodium and dietary cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork kidney is an organ meat high in arachidonic acid and purines, which amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid organ meats such as pork kidney due to high arachidonic acid and purine content, and be aware that this dish provides minimal anti-inflammatory omega-3 or antioxidant components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Kidney Meesua Soup is a traditional Singaporean Chinese noodle soup dish consisting of thin wheat vermicelli (meesua or mee sua) cooked in a savoury broth with sliced pork kidney and green vegetables. The dish is prepared by boiling the meesua noodles and pork kidney together in a seasoned broth typically flavoured with soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil. The texture is soft and silky from the fine wheat noodles, with a tender, slightly chewy pork kidney and a light, savoury broth. Meesua is a traditional Chinese wheat vermicelli associated with longevity celebrations in Hokkien and Teochew culture, and the dish is commonly consumed as a comforting meal or post-partum recovery food in Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in calories at 48 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (2.7 g), low fat (1.3 g), and moderate carbohydrate (6.6 g) per 100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 0.5 g per 100 g. Sodium is notable at 203 mg per 100 g, and a standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 812 mg sodium. The pork kidney contributes dietary cholesterol and arachidonic acid. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for patients managing hypertension (due to sodium from broth and soy sauce), type 2 diabetes (due to refined wheat noodles with low fibre and low GI, but still significant carbohydrate load), and cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis (due to organ meat cholesterol and pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060101) at confidence 0.95. The dish is a well-documented Singaporean Chinese hawker dish with an exact match in the primary regional approved source.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB source (F05060101); this is treated as a confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05060101 as of 2026. Input cultural tags (primary_cuisine: western, ethnic_tags: western, dietary_tags: vegetarian/plant_based) were corrected to Singaporean Chinese as this is a pork-containing traditional Chinese noodle soup dish. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on thin wheat vermicelli entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
